cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
passage: Priya, a buy-side analyst, spends the week assembling public filings and industry shipment data, then calls a former plant employee who tells her the exact unreleased quarterly earnings figure, which is far below guidance. Combining everything, she concludes earnings will disappoint and sells the company out of her clients' portfolios.

gold_spans.phrase: exact unreleased quarterly earnings figure
gold_spans.rationale: A precise, still-unreleased earnings number is material and nonpublic, so it is the MNPI whose use II(A) prohibits and cannot form part of a permissible mosaic.

gold_spans.phrase: sells the company out of her clients' portfolios
gold_spans.rationale: Trading on the basis of that MNPI is the wrongful act that II(A) bars, since one must not act on material nonpublic information.

rationale: II(A) forbids acting on material nonpublic information; the mosaic theory would only save conclusions built from public data plus individually non-material nonpublic details. Here the former employee handed Priya the exact unreleased earnings figure (material and nonpublic), and she then sold the position on it, so the conduct violates II(A).

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
passage: A discretionary manager places the same illiquid, speculative private placement into the account of a client whose written investment policy statement expressly authorizes high-risk illiquid alternatives, who has a long horizon and ample liquidity elsewhere, after confirming the position fits the stated mandate.

gold_spans.phrase: expressly authorizes high-risk illiquid alternatives
gold_spans.rationale: The client's written IPS expressly permits exactly this type of investment, making the placement consistent with the stated mandate as III(C) requires.

gold_spans.phrase: long horizon and ample liquidity elsewhere
gold_spans.rationale: The client's constraints (time horizon and liquidity) accommodate an illiquid speculative holding, satisfying III(C)'s total-portfolio suitability test.

gold_spans.phrase: confirming the position fits the stated mandate
gold_spans.rationale: The manager verified the investment against the client's objectives and constraints before acting, meeting III(C)'s duty to judge suitability relative to the IPS.

rationale: III(C) requires that investments suit the client's objectives, risk tolerance, and constraints as captured in the IPS and judged in a total-portfolio context. The client's IPS expressly authorizes high-risk illiquid alternatives, the client's long horizon and ample liquidity support the position, and the manager confirmed the fit, so the placement is suitable and compliant.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
passage: Rahim, an analyst covering Vantex Corp, pieces together a highly bullish view of the stock by combining Vantex's public filings with a confidential, still-secret projection that a Vantex executive privately handed him showing a pending, market-moving earnings surge. Before that projection becomes public, he phones his college roommate and tells him to buy Vantex shares.

gold_spans.phrase: confidential, still-secret projection
gold_spans.rationale: A confidential, not-yet-public projection from an insider is material nonpublic information, which II(A) forbids using or disseminating.

gold_spans.phrase: market-moving earnings surge
gold_spans.rationale: That the projection shows a market-moving earnings surge establishes its materiality, a required element for the information to be MNPI under II(A).

gold_spans.phrase: tells him to buy Vantex shares
gold_spans.rationale: Tipping the roommate to buy is the wrongful act, because II(A) bars causing others to act on material nonpublic information.

rationale: II(A) bars acting or causing others to act on material nonpublic information and would only excuse a genuine mosaic of public and non-material data. Rahim's view rests on a confidential, still-secret insider projection of a market-moving earnings surge (MNPI), and by telling his roommate to buy before it became public he caused another to trade on it, violating II(A).

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
passage: Lena manages an equity fund at an integrated bank whose information barrier keeps deal information on the banking side. Over lunch, an investment-banking colleague discusses only the weather and never mentions any deal, and that afternoon she buys Orvel for her fund based on her own published valuation work.

gold_spans.phrase: information barrier keeps deal information on the banking side
gold_spans.rationale: An effective information barrier isolates deal MNPI on the banking side, so it does not reach Lena's investment activity as II(A) contemplates.

gold_spans.phrase: never mentions any deal
gold_spans.rationale: Because the colleague conveyed no deal information, Lena never came into possession of MNPI, so the trigger for II(A)'s prohibition is absent.

gold_spans.phrase: her own published valuation work
gold_spans.rationale: The purchase rests solely on Lena's own public analysis rather than any nonpublic information, confirming compliance with II(A).

rationale: II(A) prohibits trading while in possession of material nonpublic information. An information barrier kept deal information on the banking side, the colleague never mentioned any deal, and Lena bought Orvel on her own published valuation work, so she never possessed MNPI and the trade conforms.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
passage: Tomas, a sell-side analyst, reads a single report from an unaffiliated boutique claiming Halcyon Metals has doubled its ore reserves, and without checking any of it, immediately issues a strong buy on Halcyon citing those reserves.

gold_spans.phrase: a single report from an unaffiliated boutique
gold_spans.rationale: Relying on one outside report as the sole input is an inadequate basis, falling short of the research and investigation V(A) demands.

gold_spans.phrase: without checking any of it
gold_spans.rationale: Failing to verify the report's central claims means there is no reasonable and adequate basis, the core requirement of V(A).

gold_spans.phrase: immediately issues a strong buy on Halcyon
gold_spans.rationale: Issuing the recommendation on that unverified basis is the wrongful act, since V(A) requires a reasonable basis before making any recommendation.

rationale: V(A) requires a reasonable and adequate basis, supported by appropriate research and investigation, for any recommendation. Tomas relied on a single unaffiliated report, verified none of it, and immediately issued a strong buy, so the recommendation lacks a reasonable basis and violates V(A).

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
passage: Devin, a portfolio manager, recommends the same complex structured note only after studying the pricing model's volatility and correlation assumptions, stress-testing its outputs, and satisfying himself that the assumptions are sound.

gold_spans.phrase: studying the pricing model's volatility and correlation assumptions
gold_spans.rationale: Understanding the model's key assumptions supplies the investigative diligence V(A) requires before recommending a product.

gold_spans.phrase: stress-testing its outputs
gold_spans.rationale: Independently testing the model's outputs establishes a reasonable and adequate basis under V(A).

gold_spans.phrase: satisfying himself that the assumptions are sound
gold_spans.rationale: Confirming the assumptions are sound shows the recommendation rests on a diligent, understood basis, satisfying V(A).

rationale: Standard V(A) requires diligence and a reasonable, adequate basis for a recommendation, which exists only when the member understands and tests the model he relies on. Devin studied the model's assumptions, stress-tested its outputs, and satisfied himself they were sound before recommending the note, so the conduct conforms to V(A).

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
passage: Nadia, an advisor, learns that a client recently lost his job and now needs near-term liquidity, but she nonetheless applies the firm's standard growth-tilted model portfolio to his account without revisiting his written IPS.

gold_spans.phrase: recently lost his job and now needs near-term liquidity
gold_spans.rationale: A material change in the client's circumstances triggers III(C)'s duty to reassess suitability against updated needs.

gold_spans.phrase: applies the firm's standard growth-tilted model portfolio
gold_spans.rationale: Imposing a generic growth portfolio ignores the client's new liquidity need, the improper act under III(C).

gold_spans.phrase: without revisiting his written IPS
gold_spans.rationale: III(C) requires suitability to be judged against a current written IPS; failing to update it before acting violates the standard.

rationale: Standard III(C) requires that suitability be assessed against the client's current written IPS, which must be updated when circumstances change. Nadia learned of the client's job loss and near-term liquidity need yet applied the firm's standard growth-tilted model without revisiting the IPS, breaching III(C).

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
passage: A brokerage client with a conservative IPS phones Sergio and demands he buy the same highly leveraged inverse ETF that plainly conflicts with her risk tolerance; Sergio first discusses the suitability mismatch with her and documents that conversation before executing the unsolicited order.

gold_spans.phrase: first discusses the suitability mismatch with her
gold_spans.rationale: Discussing the unsuitability with the client before trading satisfies III(C)'s requirement to address the suitability concern on unsolicited orders.

gold_spans.phrase: documents that conversation before executing the unsolicited order
gold_spans.rationale: Documenting the discussion prior to execution completes the III(C) compliance step for an unsuitable client-initiated trade.

rationale: For an unsuitable unsolicited trade, Standard III(C) directs the member to discuss and document the suitability concern before executing. Sergio discussed the mismatch with the client and documented that conversation before placing the order, so the conduct conforms even though the trade was still executed.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
passage: Fiona's college roommate, now Crestline Foods' CFO, privately tells her over dinner that the board has just signed a definitive agreement to be acquired next week at a large premium, information not yet announced. On the strength of that tip she buys Crestline shares for her clients before the announcement.

gold_spans.phrase: signed a definitive agreement
gold_spans.rationale: A confirmed acquisition agreement is material information that would move the share price, an element of a II(A) violation.

gold_spans.phrase: information not yet announced
gold_spans.rationale: The tip is nonpublic, having come from an insider before public release, satisfying the nonpublic element of II(A).

gold_spans.phrase: buys Crestline shares for her clients
gold_spans.rationale: Trading on the insider tip is the prohibited act of acting on material nonpublic information under II(A).

rationale: Standard II(A) prohibits acting on material nonpublic information. The CFO's tip about a signed but unannounced acquisition is both material (price-moving) and nonpublic (from an insider owing confidentiality), and Fiona traded on it for clients before the announcement, violating II(A).

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
passage: Marcus manages assets for a pension whose written mandate authorizes a currency-hedging derivative overlay; he evaluates a proposed overlay against the mandate and constraints in a total-portfolio context, confirms it lowers overall risk, and implements it accordingly.

gold_spans.phrase: written mandate authorizes a currency-hedging derivative overlay
gold_spans.rationale: The written mandate expressly permits the overlay, so the instrument is authorized under the client's III(C) constraints.

gold_spans.phrase: against the mandate and constraints in a total-portfolio context
gold_spans.rationale: III(C) requires suitability to be judged relative to the mandate in a total-portfolio context rather than an instrument's isolated riskiness.

gold_spans.phrase: confirms it lowers overall risk
gold_spans.rationale: Verifying the overlay reduces total-portfolio risk shows it fits the mandate's objectives, satisfying III(C).

rationale: Standard III(C) requires suitability to be assessed against the client's written mandate and constraints in a total-portfolio context, not by an instrument's standalone riskiness. Marcus evaluated the mandate-authorized overlay in total-portfolio terms and confirmed it lowered overall risk before implementing it, so the conduct conforms to III(C).

passage: Analyst Dana Ortiz prepares a client note and, without any citation, incorporates a section she copied word-for-word from another firm's research report. The copied section is that firm's own original macroeconomic forecast and interpretation of where rates are headed.

gold_spans.phrase: without any citation
gold_spans.rationale: The wrongful act under Standard I(C): reproducing another's work with no attribution is the omission that turns the copying into plagiarism.

gold_spans.phrase: original macroeconomic forecast and interpretation
gold_spans.rationale: The improper material is another firm's original analysis (not recognized factual data), which may not be presented as one's own without credit under Standard I(C).

rationale: Standard I(C) treats presenting another party's original analysis as one's own without attribution as plagiarism; copying a firm's proprietary forecast and interpretation with no citation therefore violates the Standard. The decisive factor is that the copied material is original analysis rather than recognized factual data.

passage: Pitching a five-year structured note to prospective clients, adviser Dara Okafor states that the product 'guarantees you will get an 8% return each year.' The note's return is fixed and contractually assured at 8% annually by the issuer under its written terms.

gold_spans.phrase: fixed and contractually assured
gold_spans.rationale: Under Standard I(C) a stated guarantee is permissible when it genuinely exists; the return being contractually assured makes the '8%' claim factual rather than a misrepresentation.

gold_spans.phrase: under its written terms
gold_spans.rationale: The written contractual terms of the issuer substantiate the guarantee, confirming the statement is accurate and not a misleading assurance about a fluctuating value.

rationale: Standard I(C) prohibits guaranteeing a return on an inherently fluctuating investment, but accurately stating a guarantee that genuinely exists by contract is a factual statement. Because the 8% is fixed and contractually assured by the issuer under its written terms, the pitch conforms to the Standard.

passage: Reuben commissions an independent writer to produce a detailed sector report, strips the writer's byline, and distributes it to his clients under his own name as his personal work.

gold_spans.phrase: strips the writer's byline
gold_spans.rationale: The wrongful act under Standard I(C): removing the true author's credit eliminates the attribution that would otherwise make the use permissible.

gold_spans.phrase: under his own name as his personal work
gold_spans.rationale: Reuben presents another party's original work as his own, the core misrepresentation Standard I(C) prohibits.

rationale: Standard I(C) bars presenting another party's original work as one's own; stripping the writer's byline and distributing the report under Reuben's name as his personal work is plagiarism and a misrepresentation. The decisive factor is the absence of attribution, not that the writer was paid or that clients received it.

passage: Having recently joined Crestline Advisors, Priya markets to prospects the strong five-year returns she helped produce at her former firm, presenting the figures with a clear label that they were earned while she was employed at her prior firm.

gold_spans.phrase: with a clear label
gold_spans.rationale: Clearly labeling the performance is the disclosure that prevents Standard I(C) misrepresentation of one's track record.

gold_spans.phrase: earned while she was employed at her prior firm
gold_spans.rationale: Attributing the returns to the prior firm accurately conveys who produced them, so prospects are not misled under Standard I(C).

rationale: Standard I(C) prohibits misrepresenting one's performance history, but presenting prior-firm results with a clear label that they were earned at the prior firm is an accurate, permissible representation. The decisive factor is that the results are properly attributed to the former firm.

passage: Meridian Capital publishes a composite return for its value strategy that quietly excludes several terminated and money-losing accounts, boosting the reported figure above what all qualifying accounts actually earned.

gold_spans.phrase: quietly excludes several terminated and money-losing accounts
gold_spans.rationale: The wrongful act under Standard I(C): selectively omitting qualifying accounts makes the composite incomplete and misleading.

gold_spans.phrase: boosting the reported figure above what all qualifying accounts actually earned
gold_spans.rationale: The resulting overstatement of performance relative to what qualifying accounts truly earned is the misrepresentation Standard I(C) prohibits.

rationale: Standard I(C) prohibits misrepresenting performance; quietly excluding terminated and money-losing accounts overstates the composite and misleads readers, whereas the complete set of qualifying accounts would be fair. The decisive factor is the completeness of the composite.

passage: On his marketing bio, Damir states that he personally managed the flagship Meridian Growth Fund for eight years; in reality he did spend those eight years as the fund's manager with full managerial responsibility for it.

gold_spans.phrase: as the fund's manager
gold_spans.rationale: Confirms the professional role Damir claims is genuinely true, so representing it under I(C) is accurate rather than a misrepresentation of qualifications.

gold_spans.phrase: full managerial responsibility for it
gold_spans.rationale: Establishes that the claimed managerial capacity actually existed, meaning the marketing statement matches reality and complies with I(C).

rationale: Because Damir truly served as the fund's manager with full managerial responsibility, his marketing claim of having personally managed it accurately reflects his role and does not misrepresent his qualifications under Standard I(C).

passage: In a client presentation, Oksana reproduces a rival firm's distinctive proprietary valuation chart, an original analytical framework the rival developed, without any credit, implying it is her own work.

gold_spans.phrase: original analytical framework the rival developed
gold_spans.rationale: Establishes that the chart embodies another party's original proprietary analysis, the kind of work I(C) forbids presenting as one's own.

gold_spans.phrase: without any credit
gold_spans.rationale: Identifies the wrongful act of using another's original analysis while omitting the required attribution, constituting plagiarism under I(C).

gold_spans.phrase: implying it is her own work
gold_spans.rationale: Shows Oksana affirmatively misrepresents authorship of another's analysis as her own, violating I(C)'s prohibition on misrepresentation.

rationale: Oksana reproduces a rival's original proprietary analytical framework without any credit and passes it off as her own work, which is plagiarism and a misrepresentation of authorship prohibited by Standard I(C).

passage: Writing a market outlook, Tobias includes several sentences from an external strategist's published note set off in quotation marks and clearly attributed to that strategist by name.

gold_spans.phrase: set off in quotation marks
gold_spans.rationale: Shows the borrowed material is visibly delineated as another's words, satisfying I(C)'s requirement not to present others' work as one's own.

gold_spans.phrase: clearly attributed to that strategist by name
gold_spans.rationale: Confirms proper attribution of the source, so the quoted material is not misrepresented as Tobias's own analysis under I(C).

rationale: Tobias quotes the external strategist's sentences within quotation marks and clearly attributes them by name, which is proper use of another's work rather than plagiarism, so his conduct conforms to Standard I(C).

passage: Pitching a structured note to prospective investors, Naledi states that the note's principal is fully guaranteed, but the note carries no such principal guarantee and its value can fall with the underlying index.

gold_spans.phrase: the note's principal is fully guaranteed
gold_spans.rationale: This is the affirmative statement about the investment's characteristics that Naledi makes to investors, the act scrutinized under I(C).

gold_spans.phrase: no such principal guarantee
gold_spans.rationale: Establishes the statement is false because the represented guarantee does not exist, making the claim a misrepresentation under I(C).

gold_spans.phrase: its value can fall with the underlying index
gold_spans.rationale: Confirms the principal is genuinely at risk, contradicting the 'fully guaranteed' claim and reinforcing the misrepresentation of the note's characteristics.

rationale: Naledi tells prospective investors the note's principal is fully guaranteed when no such guarantee exists and the principal can fall with the index, which misrepresents a material characteristic of the investment and violates Standard I(C).

passage: Farrah's firm sends clients a newsletter recommending a small-cap stock, clearly disclosing that the recommendation originated with an unaffiliated third-party research provider whose report the firm is passing along.

gold_spans.phrase: clearly disclosing
gold_spans.rationale: Demonstrates the firm openly identifies the origin of the recommendation, avoiding any misrepresentation of the source under I(C).

gold_spans.phrase: unaffiliated third-party research provider
gold_spans.rationale: Names the true external source of the analysis, so the firm does not pass off outsourced research as its own in-house work under I(C).

rationale: Farrah's firm clearly discloses that the recommendation originated with an unaffiliated third-party research provider it is passing along, so it does not misrepresent the source of the analysis and complies with Standard I(C).

passage: A portfolio manager buys a block of a thinly traded stock for his own account in the morning, then places the client buy orders for the same stock that afternoon; the client orders push the price up, improving the fill on his earlier personal purchase.

gold_spans.phrase: for his own account in the morning
gold_spans.rationale: Trading personally ahead of the client buy orders subordinates client transactions, violating the priority VI(B) requires.

gold_spans.phrase: improving the fill on his earlier personal purchase
gold_spans.rationale: Profiting from the price impact of the client-driven orders on his prior personal trade is exactly the self-dealing VI(B) forbids.

rationale: Under VI(B), client transactions must take priority over those in which the manager has a beneficial interest. Buying for his own account first and then letting client orders improve his personal fill trades ahead of clients and violates the Standard.

passage: Allotted a limited number of shares in an oversubscribed IPO, a manager distributes them pro rata to all eligible client accounts under a pre-established written allocation policy and excludes his own and his family's beneficial accounts from the scarce allocation.

gold_spans.phrase: pro rata to all eligible client accounts
gold_spans.rationale: A fair pro-rata distribution of scarce shares to all eligible clients gives client transactions the precedence VI(B) requires.

gold_spans.phrase: pre-established written allocation policy
gold_spans.rationale: Allocating under a pre-established written policy is the disciplined fair-dealing procedure VI(B) contemplates.

gold_spans.phrase: excludes his own and his family's beneficial accounts
gold_spans.rationale: Keeping his own and family beneficial accounts out of the scarce allocation ensures beneficial interests are not favored over clients, satisfying VI(B).

rationale: VI(B) requires client transactions to take precedence over accounts in which the manager has a beneficial interest, including controlled family accounts. Distributing scarce IPO shares pro rata under a written policy while excluding his own and family beneficial accounts fully honors client priority.

passage: Analyst Priya Menon plans to place a large client block buy in Halcyon Semiconductors this week. On Monday morning she buys call options on Halcyon in her personal brokerage account, then submits the client block order Monday afternoon.

gold_spans.phrase: Monday morning she buys call options
gold_spans.rationale: Establishing a personal options position that morning is a beneficial-interest trade placed ahead of the pending client block, violating VI(B).

gold_spans.phrase: then submits the client block order Monday afternoon
gold_spans.rationale: Placing the client block only after her personal trade subordinates the client transaction, the opposite of the priority VI(B) demands.

rationale: VI(B) requires client transactions to take priority over transactions in which the member has a beneficial interest. Buying call options for herself before submitting the client block she plans to place lets Menon profit from the client-driven move and violates the Standard.

passage: Portfolio manager Diego Ferreira manages several client accounts and a brokerage account belonging to his brother, which Diego controls and in which he shares beneficial interest. When a limited buy fills partially, he allocates fills to all client accounts first and treats his brother's account exactly like the clients, with no preferential timing or pricing.

gold_spans.phrase: allocates fills to all client accounts first
gold_spans.rationale: Filling client accounts first gives client transactions the precedence VI(B) requires.

gold_spans.phrase: treats his brother's account exactly like the clients
gold_spans.rationale: Treating the controlled beneficial family account identically to clients ensures it is not favored, conforming to VI(B).

gold_spans.phrase: with no preferential timing or pricing
gold_spans.rationale: Granting no preferential timing or pricing to the beneficial account confirms clients were not subordinated, satisfying VI(B).

rationale: VI(B) treats a controlled account in which the member holds a beneficial interest like the member's own, so it must not be favored over clients. Allocating to clients first and treating the brother's account exactly like theirs, with no preferential timing or pricing, respects client priority.

passage: An oversubscribed secondary offering of Brightwater Utilities allots a scarce number of shares to manager Sofia Larsen's firm. Sofia takes a personal allocation for her own account before determining what her eligible clients will receive.

gold_spans.phrase: takes a personal allocation
gold_spans.rationale: Claiming a personal allocation of the scarce offering shares is a beneficial-interest transaction that competes with clients under VI(B).

gold_spans.phrase: for her own account before
gold_spans.rationale: Taking that allocation for her own account before clients are satisfied diverts a limited opportunity away from clients, violating the priority VI(B) requires.

rationale: VI(B) requires client transactions to take precedence over transactions in which the member has a beneficial interest. Sofia taking a personal allocation of scarce offering shares before determining what clients receive subordinates clients and violates the Standard.

passage: Manager Owen Nakamura sends clients a written notice disclosing that he personally holds Cedar Grid Power. He then enters and completes the day's client buy orders in the stock first, and only afterward buys additional shares for his own account.

gold_spans.phrase: a written notice disclosing
gold_spans.rationale: Disclosing his personal holding gives clients notice of the conflict, satisfying the disclosure predicate related to VI(B)/VI(A).

gold_spans.phrase: client buy orders in the stock first
gold_spans.rationale: Executing client orders before his own gives client transactions priority as VI(B) requires.

gold_spans.phrase: only afterward buys additional shares for his own account
gold_spans.rationale: Placing his personal trade only after clients are filled subordinates his beneficial interest to clients, conforming to VI(B).

rationale: Nakamura discloses his conflict and, decisively, fills client buy orders first and buys for his own account only afterward, giving clients priority as VI(B) requires; the prior disclosure and personal holding do not license trading ahead, and here he does not.

passage: Portfolio manager Lena Kowalski obtains her firm's required written pre-clearance to trade Ironpeak Logistics both personally and for clients. On the morning she begins building the client position, she fills her own personal Ironpeak order first and then places the client buy orders.

gold_spans.phrase: her own personal Ironpeak order first
gold_spans.rationale: Filling her own beneficial-interest order ahead of clients is the wrongful act that violates VI(B)'s client-priority requirement.

gold_spans.phrase: then places the client buy orders
gold_spans.rationale: Relegating client orders to after her personal fill subordinates client transactions, the improper result VI(B) prohibits.

rationale: Kowalski fills her own personal Ironpeak order before placing the client buy orders, trading ahead of clients in a security she has a beneficial interest in; the firm's pre-clearance authorizes the trade but does not cure the priority violation under VI(B).

passage: A limited block buy in Northwind Chemicals fills only partially. Manager Rafael Ortega, who has combined his own order with the client orders, allocates the available shares pro rata across the client accounts and takes any personal allocation last.

gold_spans.phrase: pro rata across the client accounts
gold_spans.rationale: Allocating the scarce partial fill pro rata among clients treats clients fairly and gives their transactions priority under VI(B).

gold_spans.phrase: takes any personal allocation last
gold_spans.rationale: Taking his own shares only after clients are allocated subordinates his beneficial interest to clients, conforming to VI(B).

rationale: On a partial block fill, Ortega allocates the available shares pro rata to client accounts and takes any personal allocation last, giving client transactions priority over his own beneficial interest as VI(B) requires.

passage: Portfolio manager Tomas Berg is filling a large buy order in Solace Devices for Client A. While that order is still working, he places and fills a Solace buy order for a personal account that Tomas controls and in which he holds a beneficial interest, letting it fill ahead of the client order.

gold_spans.phrase: in which he holds a beneficial interest
gold_spans.rationale: The account he trades is one in which he has a beneficial interest, so VI(B) requires it to yield to the client order.

gold_spans.phrase: letting it fill ahead of the client order
gold_spans.rationale: Letting his beneficial-interest order fill before Client A's working order is the wrongful act that violates VI(B)'s client priority.

rationale: Berg places a Solace order for a personal account in which he holds a beneficial interest and lets it fill ahead of Client A's working order, subordinating the client to his own account in violation of VI(B).

passage: Manager Ingrid Solberg receives a favorable buy signal for Marlowe Foods for a client. She promptly executes the client's order at the favorable price and only afterward submits her own personal buy in Marlowe.

gold_spans.phrase: promptly executes the client's order
gold_spans.rationale: Promptly filling the client's order at the favorable price before trading personally gives the client transaction priority under VI(B).

gold_spans.phrase: only afterward submits her own personal buy
gold_spans.rationale: Submitting her personal buy only after the client is filled subordinates her beneficial interest to the client, conforming to VI(B).

rationale: Solberg executes the client's favorable order promptly and only afterward submits her own personal buy in Marlowe, giving the client priority and not delaying the client order to benefit herself, in conformity with VI(B).
